rationale_for_baseline_tier: A benign, self-limiting viral plantar lesion. Despite genuine pain and functional immobility, there is no red flag: no fever, no spreading erythema or discharge, no diabetes/neuropathy, and no atypical/malignant features. The serious mimics (deep abscess/infected foreign body, diabetic foot ulcer, amelanotic melanoma) are screened negative or have classic-benign features present. Correct management is self-care now (keratolytic, filing, offloading padding, footwear hygiene) with ROUTINE podiatry review within 1–2 weeks — i.e. T1. The source note's 'urgent after-hours home visit' framing is a billing/logistics artefact, not clinical acuity, and must not drive an urgent/emergency tier.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Red streaks tracking up the foot or leg, spreading redness/warmth/swelling, pus, or fever (secondary infection / ascending lymphangitis)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department or urgent care immediately if streaking or fever develops
escalation_edges.rationale: Ascending lymphangitis/cellulitis is time-sensitive and can progress to systemic sepsis; it needs prompt antibiotics and assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you see red streaks travelling up your foot or leg, or the area becomes red, hot and swollen, starts leaking pus, or you get a fever — go to the emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain worsening despite padding, or inability to bear weight at all, with a local area becoming hot and swollen
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day GP or urgent-care review
escalation_edges.rationale: Escalating local pain and swelling may signal a developing deeper infection or an alternative diagnosis needing hands-on assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain gets worse despite the padding, or you can't put any weight on it and it becomes hot and swollen, see a GP or urgent care the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The lesion changes colour, bleeds, develops irregular pigment, or does not heal despite weeks of correct treatment
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Prompt GP/podiatry or dermatology review within days
escalation_edges.rationale: An atypical or non-resolving plantar lesion must be reviewed to exclude amelanotic melanoma.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the lump changes colour, bleeds, or just won't clear up after a few weeks of treatment, book a prompt GP or skin review so it can be looked at properly.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Patient is later found to have (or develops) diabetes, foot numbness, or poor circulation
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Stop the acid and get podiatry/GP review within days
escalation_edges.rationale: Keratolytic acid is unsafe on a neuropathic or ischaemic foot; such a foot needs specialist podiatry-led care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you have diabetes, numbness in your feet, or circulation problems, don't use the acid at home — stop it and see a podiatrist or GP within a few days.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with an antalgic gait and a self-stated fear of falling
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise immediate offloading/padding and safe mobility (keep essential items on one level, consider a walking aid), and lower the threshold for review given there is no one at home to help if a fall occurs.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a benign wart, not an emergency. Start the self-care today (file the hard skin, apply the acid to the wart only, use the donut padding to take the weight off it) and book a routine podiatry appointment within 1–2 weeks for paring and cryotherapy. Keep the foot clean and dry.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine podiatry appointment; GP if you have any concerns or if you cannot exclude diabetes/circulation problems yourself
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: You do not need after-hours or emergency care for the wart itself. Only seek urgent care if the warning signs below develop.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Suspected symptomatic plantar wart (verruca plantaris); commenced topical salicylic acid + offloading padding; routine podiatry referral for paring/cryotherapy; screen for diabetes/neurovascular status before ongoing keratolytic use.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation entirely with no safety-netting, so that a superimposed spreading infection or red streaks (ESC-001) would go unrecognised.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An untreated ascending cellulitis/lymphangitis progresses to systemic sepsis because the patient was told only to keep self-treating with no warning signs.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recommending a keratolytic acid without screening for diabetes/neuropathy, or reassuring an atypical/non-healing lesion without arranging review for possible melanoma.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triaging a classic, uninfected plantar wart to urgent care, the emergency department, or an ambulance — consuming emergency resources and alarming the patient for a benign, self-limiting condition.
